what: ATE: average treatment effect in the whole population
title: Getting started with YLLgmethods
text: estimate_yll_gformula_ate() thin wrapper contrasts “everyone reference” vs. “everyone exposed” averages entire study population. result object always contains: summary — one row per starting age point estimate, CI, CI method detailed_results — point estimates plus per-arm life expectancy percentile normal-approximation CIs (B > 0) meta — call metadata (estimand, B, conf_level, integration, …) marginal_survival_point marginal_survival_boot — marginal survival curves used plot_*() helpers
code: res_ate <- estimate_yll_gformula_ate(   data = yll_toy,   B = 50,   method = "normal",   show_progress = FALSE,   id_var = "id",   time_var = "period",   event_var = "event",   exposure_var = "hypertension",   reference_level = "No",   exposed_level = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var = "age",   age_start = 50,   age_end = 90,   age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary"),   use_future = FALSE )  res_ate$summary

title: Getting started with YLLgmethods
text: _att() averages contrast observed exposed, _atc() observed reference level. arguments, different target population:
code: res_att <- estimate_yll_gformula_att(   data = yll_toy,   B = 50, method = "normal", show_progress = FALSE, use_future = FALSE,   id_var = "id", time_var = "period", event_var = "event",   exposure_var = "hypertension",   reference_level = "No", exposed_level = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var = "age",   age_start = 50, age_end = 90, age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary") )  res_atc <- estimate_yll_gformula_atc(   data = yll_toy,   B = 50, method = "normal", show_progress = FALSE, use_future = FALSE,   id_var = "id", time_var = "period", event_var = "event",   exposure_var = "hypertension",   reference_level = "No", exposed_level = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var = "age",   age_start = 50, age_end = 90, age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary") )

title: Getting started with YLLgmethods
text: policy-relevant questions “set everyone exposed” unrealistic, use stochastic intervention shifts exposure distribution. convenience wrapper estimate_yll_gformula_binary_stochastic_vs_natural() compares target intervention natural exposure distribution observed data. example contrasts world 20% unexposed 100% exposed end exposed natural distribution: See vignette("interventional-effects", package = "YLLgmethods") underlying theory examples.
code: res_increase <- estimate_yll_gformula_binary_stochastic_vs_natural(   data = yll_toy,   prob_exposed_if_unexposed = 0.2,   prob_exposed_if_exposed   = 1.0,   target_population = NULL,   B = 50, show_progress = FALSE, use_future = FALSE,   id_var = "id", time_var = "period", event_var = "event",   exposure_var = "hypertension",   reference_level = "No", exposed_level = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var = "age",   age_start = 50, age_end = 90, age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary") )

title: Getting started with YLLgmethods
text: Three plotting helpers included; return ggplot2 objects can post-process freely.
code: plot_yll(res_ate) plot_conditional_survival(res_ate, age_start = 60) plot_marginal_survival(res_ate)

title: Getting started with YLLgmethods
text: Set seed make bootstrap deterministic. Set use_future = TRUE configure future::plan() parallelise bootstrap. integration = "trapezoidal" produces slightly smoother LE estimates; default "left_rectangle" consistent discrete-time hazard model.

what: 1. From static interventions to a more general framework
title: Generalized interventional effects with YLLgmethods
text: classical average treatment effect (ATE) compares two static worlds, e.g. everyone non-smoker vs. everyone current smoker: ATE=E[Ya=1]−E[Ya=0]. \mathrm{ATE} = E[Y^{=1}] - E[Y^{=0}]. conceptually clean often unrealistic. Generalized interventional effects instead define effect class interventions — typically specifying stochastic assignment distribution g(∣L)g(\mid L) contrasting two distributions: E[Yg1]−E[Yg0]. E[Y^{g_1}] - E[Y^{g_0}]. g-formula identifies E[Yg]=EL[∑aE[Y∣=,L]g(∣L)]. E[Y^g] = E_L\!\left[\, \sum_a E[Y \mid = , L]\, g(\mid L) \right].

title: Generalized interventional effects with YLLgmethods
text: Suppose observed exposure binary, ∈{reference,exposed}\\{\text{reference}, \text{exposed}\}, want contrast: Target intervention. “Among observed unexposed, 20% become exposed; among observed exposed, stay exposed.” Reference intervention. shift (natural exposure distribution). subject g-formula needs P(*=exposed∣,L)P(^* = \text{exposed} \mid , L) two regimes. YLLgmethods exposes : yll_make_binary_stochastic_intervention() — builds function f(data) returning subject-specific exposure probabilities, indexed subject’s originally observed exposure status. estimate_yll_gformula_intervention() — takes intervention_reference intervention_exposed, either scalar probability, vector probabilities, exposure-level label, function built helper . estimate_yll_gformula_binary_stochastic() ..._binary_stochastic_vs_natural() — convenience wrappers around common binary-exposure shifts.

title: Generalized interventional effects with YLLgmethods
text: Imagine public-health programme , applied uniformly unexposed group, expose 20% keep observed-exposed subjects exposed. compare natural exposure distribution produced observed data. “natural” intervention reuses empirical exposure assignments, subjects observed Yes get P(*=Yes)=1P(^* = \text{Yes}) = 1 observed get 00. Internally just degenerate stochastic intervention — exactly machinery.
code: library(YLLgmethods) data(yll_toy)  res <- estimate_yll_gformula_binary_stochastic_vs_natural(   data = yll_toy,   prob_exposed_if_unexposed = 0.20,   prob_exposed_if_exposed   = 1.00,   target_population = NULL,        # average over the entire population   B = 50, show_progress = FALSE, use_future = FALSE,   id_var = "id", time_var = "period", event_var = "event",   exposure_var = "hypertension",   reference_level = "No", exposed_level = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var = "age",   age_start = 50, age_end = 90, age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary") )  res$summary

title: Generalized interventional effects with YLLgmethods
text: arbitrary regimes (covariate-dependent assignment, dynamic rules, shift-based interventions, …), pass function directly estimate_yll_gformula_intervention(): intervention_* arguments accept: scalar probability [0, 1], vector length nrow(data) probabilities, value matching one observed exposure levels (treated degenerate intervention), function f(data) returning either .
code: intervention_target <- function(data) {   # Higher exposure probability for older subjects   plogis(-1 + 0.05 * (data$age - 55)) }  res <- estimate_yll_gformula_intervention(   data = yll_toy,   intervention_reference = "No",        # natural reference: nobody exposed   intervention_exposed   = intervention_target,   id_var = "id", time_var = "period", event_var = "event",   exposure_var = "hypertension",   reference_level = "No", exposed_level = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var = "age",   age_start = 50, age_end = 90, age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary"),   B = 0, use_future = FALSE )

title: Generalized interventional effects with YLLgmethods
text: estimands recover true counterfactual contrast, usual g-formula conditions required: Consistency counterfactual outcomes hypothesised regimes. Conditional exchangeability Ya⊥∣LY^\perp \mid L. Positivity shifted exposure regimes given chosen conditioning set. correctly specified outcome model — discrete-time pooled logistic hazard natural cubic splines attained age. Stochastic interventions particularly attractive fully static regimes infeasible violate positivity, shifts can designed stay within empirical support data.

code: @Manual{,   title = {YLLgmethods: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula},   author = {Shuntaro Sato},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/ShuntaroS/yll_g-methods}, }

what: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
text: Years life lost (YLL) counterfactual life expectancy user-specified interventions binary exposure, estimated parametric g-formula attained-age time scale. package supports: ATE / ATT / ATC contrasts binary exposure. Arbitrary deterministic, stochastic, covariate-dependent interventions small intervention API. Bootstrap confidence intervals (normal-approximation percentile), optionally parallelised future. Plotting helpers marginal conditional survival curves YLL across starting ages.

title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
text: Full reference vignettes available package website: https://shuntaros.github.io/yll_g-methods/ Function reference Getting started Generalized interventional effects

title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
text: package currently distributed GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("ShuntaroS/yll_g-methods")

title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
text: See vignette("getting-started", package = "YLLgmethods") tour user-facing functions, vignette("interventional-effects", package = "YLLgmethods") identification framework behind stochastic dynamic interventions.
code: library(YLLgmethods) data(yll_toy)  res <- estimate_yll_gformula_ate(   data = yll_toy,   id_var = "id",   time_var = "period",   event_var = "event",   exposure_var = "hypertension",   reference_level = "No",   exposed_level = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var = "age",   age_start = 50,   age_end = 90,   age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary"),   B = 200,   method = "normal",   use_future = FALSE )  res$summary plot_yll(res)

title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
text: Every estimate_yll_gformula_*() function returns list : method selects normal-approximation ("normal", default) percentile ("percentile") bootstrap CIs; res$summary$ci_method records one used.

title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
text: Estimands: estimate_yll_gformula_ate() / _att() / _atc() — binary-exposure contrasts averaged chosen target population. estimate_yll_gformula_intervention() — generic interventional contrast accepting either fixed exposure values function f(data) returning subject-specific exposure probabilities. estimate_yll_gformula_binary_stochastic() estimate_yll_gformula_binary_stochastic_vs_natural() — convenience wrappers binary stochastic shifts, latter using natural exposure distribution reference. Helpers: yll_make_binary_stochastic_intervention() — build intervention function (P(^*=\text{exposed}\mid =\text{ref}), P(^*=\text{exposed}\mid =\text{exp})). plot_yll(), plot_marginal_survival(), plot_conditional_survival() — ggplot2-based plots result object.

title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula
text: current implementation binary exposures. Multi-level continuous exposures yet supported. Variable entry age introduces left-truncation / delayed-entry problem current implementation address rigorously; treat results datasets variable age_at_entry_var preliminary.

title: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula

what: YLLgmethods: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula — YLLgmethods-package
title: YLLgmethods: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula — YLLgmethods-package
text: Estimates Years Life Lost (YLL) counterfactual life expectancy user-specified interventions binary exposure using parametric g-formula attained-age time scale. Supports ATE, ATT, ATC, stochastic / population interventions, bootstrap confidence intervals helpers plotting conditional marginal survival curves.

title: YLLgmethods: Years of Life Lost via the G-Formula — YLLgmethods-package
text: Maintainer: Shuntaro Sato shuntaro.sato.stat@gmail.com (ORCID)

what: Estimate YLL via the g-formula (ATE / ATT / ATC) — estimate_yll_gformula
title: Estimate YLL via the g-formula (ATE / ATT / ATC) — estimate_yll_gformula
text: Fits discrete-time hazard model attained-age time scale, builds counterfactual survival curves "reference" vs. "exposed" interventions, reports Years Life Lost (YLL) counterfactual life expectancy (LE) user-chosen starting ages, bootstrap confidence intervals.

title: Estimate YLL via the g-formula (ATE / ATT / ATC) — estimate_yll_gformula
code: estimate_yll_gformula(   data,   B = 1000,   seed = 1,   conf_level = 0.95,   method = c("normal", "percentile"),   show_progress = TRUE,   id_var = "id",   time_var,   event_var,   exposure_var,   reference_level = NULL,   exposed_level = NULL,   age_at_entry_var,   age_start = 50,   age_end = 100,   age_interval = 5,   confounders_baseline = NULL,   estimand = c("ATT", "ATC", "ATE"),   integration = c("left_rectangle", "trapezoidal"),   use_future = TRUE )

title: Estimate YLL via the g-formula (ATE / ATT / ATC) — estimate_yll_gformula
text: data data.frame one row per individual, containing variables specified id_var, time_var, event_var, exposure_var, age_at_entry_var, confounders_baseline. B Number bootstrap iterations. Set 0 skip bootstrapping (returns point estimates ). seed Integer seed reproducibility. conf_level Confidence level intervals (e.g. 0.95). method Either "normal" (Wald-type interval using bootstrap SE) "percentile" (bootstrap percentile interval). show_progress Show progress bar bootstrap. id_var, time_var, event_var, exposure_var Column names data. reference_level, exposed_level Values labelling two exposure levels. NULL inferred data. age_at_entry_var Column name age study entry. age_start, age_end, age_interval Starting ages YLL reported, given seq(age_start, age_end, age_interval). confounders_baseline Character vector baseline confounder column names. Including entry-age column discouraged triggers warning, since attained age already modelled via splines. estimand One "ATT", "ATC", "ATE". integration Discretisation rule area conditional survival curve: "left_rectangle" (default; matches discrete-time hazard model) "trapezoidal". use_future TRUE, parallelise bootstrap future.apply::future_lapply(). caller expected set parallel plan (e.g. future::plan(future::multisession())).

title: Estimate YLL via the g-formula (ATE / ATT / ATC) — estimate_yll_gformula
text: list elements summary Point estimates CI YLL starting age. detailed_results summary plus LE arm CI / SE columns. meta Run settings (B, seed, conf_level, method, estimand, age_start, age_end, age_interval, integration). marginal_survival_point Population-marginal counterfactual survival curves (point estimate). marginal_survival_boot curves across bootstrap iterations (used plotting helpers).

title: Estimate YLL via the g-formula (ATE / ATT / ATC) — estimate_yll_gformula
text: function main legacy interface. arbitrary interventions target populations, use estimate_yll_gformula_intervention().

title: Estimate YLL via the g-formula (ATE / ATT / ATC) — estimate_yll_gformula
code: # \donttest{ data(yll_toy, package = "YLLgmethods") res <- estimate_yll_gformula_ate(   data                 = yll_toy,   B                    = 0,   show_progress        = FALSE,   time_var             = "period",   event_var            = "event",   exposure_var         = "hypertension",   reference_level      = "No",   exposed_level        = "Yes",   age_at_entry_var     = "age",   age_start            = 50,   age_end              = 80,   age_interval         = 10,   confounders_baseline = c("sex", "education_years", "bmi", "smoke_binary"),   use_future           = FALSE ) #> Warning: B = 0 so confidence intervals were not computed. res$summary #> # A tibble: 4 × 5 #>   starting_age   yll ci_low ci_high ci_method #>          <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>     #> 1           50 2.28      NA      NA normal    #> 2           60 1.78      NA      NA normal    #> 3           70 0.822     NA      NA normal    #> 4           80 0         NA      NA normal    # }

what: Synthetic survival dataset for YLL examples — yll_toy
title: Synthetic survival dataset for YLL examples — yll_toy
text: simulated cohort used demonstrate package functions. row one individual followed 15 years, binary smoking exposure, binary hypertension covariate, continuous-time--event outcome. data-generating process encoded data-raw/yll_toy.R.

title: Synthetic survival dataset for YLL examples — yll_toy
code: yll_toy

title: Synthetic survival dataset for YLL examples — yll_toy
text: data frame 5,000 rows 9 variables: id Integer subject identifier (1..n). period Numeric follow-time, years, study entry event administrative censoring year 15. event Integer event indicator: 1 death, 0 censored. smoke_binary Factor exposure levels "Never" "Current/Ever". hypertension Factor confounder levels "" "Yes". sex Factor levels "Female" "Male". education_years Integer years completed education (9-20). bmi Numeric body-mass index entry (16.0-40.0). age Integer age years study entry (35-69).

title: Synthetic survival dataset for YLL examples — yll_toy
text: Simulated. See data-raw/yll_toy.R data-generating script.
